prompt: People often have different impressions of a country. What are two words you would use to describe British culture or British people? Please explain why.
reference_answer:
10	I would definitely describe British culture using the words 'polite' and 'historic'. Firstly, British people are globally recognized for their impeccable manners, constantly apologizing and strictly adhering to queuing rules. Secondly, the nation is profoundly historic, which is evident in their centuries-old architecture and the enduring traditions of the royal family. I believe these two traits perfectly capture the essence of the UK.|If I had to choose, my two words would be 'reserved' and 'traditional'. British individuals often appear reserved initially because they highly value personal space and tend to avoid loud conversations in public. Furthermore, the culture remains deeply traditional, successfully preserving timeless customs such as formal afternoon tea and elaborate monarchy ceremonies.|I would use the words 'gentlemanly' and 'diverse' to describe the country. Historically, British men are often stereotyped as well-dressed gentlemen who carry umbrellas and exhibit chivalrous behavior. However, modern British culture is also incredibly diverse, especially in major cities like London, where you can experience a vibrant mix of global cuisines and cultures.
9	I would use the words 'polite' and 'historic' to describe them. British people are very polite, they always say sorry and line up for everything in the street. Also, the UK is very historic country with many old buildings and traditions like the royal family.|My two words is 'traditional' and 'reserved'. British people like to keep their old customs, like drinking tea every afternoon with friends. They also look a bit reserved to strangers, but actually they are very friendly when you start talking to them.|I will say 'gentleman' and 'rainy'. The people there are very polite and they always wear nice clothes like a gentleman. The weather in the UK is very famous for raining, so people always bring a umbrella with them.
8	My two words are 'polite' and 'traditional'. I think British people is very polite because they always say thank you and follow the rules. The culture is traditional because they like to drink afternoon tea and they still have a king.|I choose 'polite' and 'tea'. British people very like to drink tea everyday in the afternoon. They are also polite people, because they don't speak very loud in the public place.|I will use 'gentleman' and 'rain'. English men is always look like gentleman in the movie. And the weather always rain, so it is a very rainy country.
7	I choose 'polite' and 'rainy'. British people are polite to others and they always line up. Also, the weather in the UK is always raining.|I think is 'tea' and 'good'. They drink many black tea everyday. The people is good and friendly to the tourists.|My words are 'old' and 'polite'. They have many old building in London. And they are polite when they talk to you.
6	I will say polite and rainy. Because British people always saying sorry. And it rain a lot in the UK.|Polite and tea. They are very polite people. They drinking tea in afternoon.|It is old and polite. The country is very old. People is polite to me.
5	I choose polite and rain. British people is very good. They drinking tea everyday.|My word is gentleman and old. The country have long history. Weather is rain.|I think polite and tea. They say sorry much. Drink tea in afternoon.
4	Polite. And rain. People is say sorry.|Old country. Tea is good. I like.|Gentleman. Wear suit. Rain umbrella.
3	People is polite... rain.|Drink tea... good.|Old... building.
2	Polite.|UK rain.|Tea.
1	I don't know.|(Silence)|Uh...

prompt: If you have the chance to visit one famous place in the UK, where would you go? What do you expect to see or do there?
reference_answer:
10	If given the opportunity, I would undeniably choose to visit the British Museum in London. As a history enthusiast, I eagerly expect to explore its massive collection of global artifacts, particularly the Rosetta Stone and the Egyptian mummies. Walking through those halls would be a profoundly enriching and educational experience for me.|I would absolutely love to explore the Tower of London during my visit. I expect to participate in a guided tour led by a traditional Beefeater to uncover the castle's fascinating and bloody history. Furthermore, getting a close look at the spectacular Crown Jewels is something I have dreamed of doing since I was a child.|My primary destination would be the London Eye because I am captivated by stunning cityscapes. I expect to take a leisurely ride in one of the glass capsules to admire the panoramic view of the entire capital. It would be an unforgettable moment to photograph the River Thames and the Houses of Parliament from such a magnificent height.
9	If I have the chance, I want to visit the London Eye. I expect to take a ride on it and enjoy the beautiful view of the whole city from high above. It would be amazing to see the River Thames and Big Ben from the top.|I want to go to the British Museum because I like history very much. I expect to see many famous historical things from different countries. It will be a great chance to learn about ancient Egypt and Greece.|I will choose Big Ben and the Parliament. I expect to hear the famous bell ringing and take a lot of pictures. It is a very iconic landmark, so I must go there to see it with my own eyes.
8	I would like to visit Big Ben in London. It is a very famous clock tower in the world. I want to take a lot of beautiful picture of it and walk around the street nearby.|I want to go the British Museum. Because it have many old things from the world. I expect to see the mummies and taking photos with them.|I will visit London Eye. It is a very big Ferris wheel. I can sit inside and seeing the whole London city, it will be very beautiful.
7	I want to go to the London Eye. Because it is very big and famous. I can see the beautiful city view from there.|I will go to Big Ben. It is a big clock in London. I expect to take photos with my friends there.|I choose the British Museum. There are many old things to see. It is very interesting and I can learn history.
6	I want to go to Big Ben. It is a very big clock. I will taking many photos there.|London Eye is my choice. It is very high. I want to looking the city.|I go to British Museum. It has many old thing. I like it.
5	I want go Big Ben. It is big clock. I taking picture.|London Eye. It is very high. I can looking the city.|I go British Museum. Many old thing inside. Very fun.
4	Go Big Ben. Picture.|London Eye. See city.|Museum. Look old thing.
3	Big clock... go.|London Eye... high.|Museum... history.
2	Big Ben.|London.|Museum.
1	No.|(Silence)|Hmm...

prompt: Visiting second-hand markets is a popular weekend activity in the UK. How do you feel about buying second-hand items? What kind of special things do you hope to find in a British market?
reference_answer:
10	I harbor a highly positive attitude toward shopping for second-hand items because it effectively promotes environmental sustainability and personal savings. By purchasing pre-owned goods, we actively participate in reducing unnecessary global waste. If I were to wander through a British market, I would earnestly hope to discover rare vintage clothing or classic vinyl records that possess a unique historical charm.|I truly believe that purchasing second-hand items is a brilliant concept because it allows you to unearth hidden treasures at remarkably affordable prices. It perfectly aligns with the modern trend of eco-friendly living. While browsing a traditional British market, I would be thrilled to find authentic English porcelain teacups or beautifully preserved vintage postcards.|Exploring second-hand markets is genuinely one of my favorite leisure activities, primarily because every single item carries its own mysterious backstory. It essentially grants old objects a meaningful second life instead of ending up in a landfill. In a UK market, I would eagerly hunt for classic English literature or perhaps an elegant, antique pocket watch.
9	I think buying second-hand items is a wonderful idea because you can discover hidden treasures at a bargain price. It is also very good for the environment. If I go to a British market, I would love to find traditional English teacups or vintage postcards to take home as souvenirs.|I feel good about buying second-hand things because it can save money and reduce waste. In a British market, I hope to find some special vintage clothes or old cameras. It is very exciting to find something special that nobody else has.|I like to buy second-hand items. It is very eco-friendly and cheap. When I go to a British market, I hope I can find some old vinyl records or silver accessories. They have a very classic style that I like very much.
8	I like buying second-hand things very much. It is fun and can save a lot of money. In the UK market, I hope to find some cool vintage jacket or interesting old books.|Buying second-hand item is good. Because we can protect the earth. I hope to find some traditional English tea cups and old coins in the market.|I feel very happy to buy second-hand things. They are very cheap. In the British market, I want to find some special old clothes and bags.
7	I feel good about buying second-hand items because they are cheap. If I go to the market, I want to find some old clothes and beautiful cups.|I like second-hand things. It can save money. I hope to find some old books and records in the UK market.|Buying second-hand is a good idea. The things are cheap and special. I want to buy old clothes.
6	I think it is good. I like cheap thing. I hope to buy some old clothes in the UK market.|Second-hand is good. I can saving money. I want to find old books.|It is very fun. I like old things. I hope to buying cups.
5	I think it is good. I buying cheap clothes. Find old book.|Very good. Can save money. I want buy cup.|I like second hand. It is cheap. Find special thing.
4	Good. Buy old clothes.|Cheap. Save money.|Market fun. Buy cup.

prompt: British afternoon tea is famous worldwide. Imagine you are having a traditional afternoon tea in London. What items do you expect to see on the table, and how is the atmosphere?
reference_answer:
10	If I were experiencing a traditional afternoon tea in London, I would anticipate seeing an elegant three-tier stand proudly positioned in the center of the table. This exquisite stand would be laden with crustless finger sandwiches, freshly baked warm scones, and intricately decorated pastries. The atmosphere would undoubtedly be sophisticated and tranquil, likely accompanied by the soothing sounds of live classical music.|On the beautifully set table, I would expect to find a delicate floral porcelain tea set alongside freshly baked scones, complete with generous portions of clotted cream and strawberry jam. Additionally, there would be a diverse selection of premium loose-leaf teas. The overall ambiance would feel incredibly cozy yet refined, providing the perfect environment for intimate conversations.|I would completely expect a lavish presentation featuring a polished silver teapot, fine china, and a tiered tray overflowing with macarons, sandwiches, and traditional scones. Authentic clotted cream is an absolute necessity for this experience. The atmosphere in the tea room would be luxurious but relaxing, allowing guests to leisurely enjoy their afternoon in style.
9	On the table, I expect to see a beautiful tea set, freshly baked scones with thick cream and jam, and various small cakes. There should also be a pot of hot black tea. The atmosphere would feel very traditional and cozy, giving people a chance to chat and relax in a fancy room.|I expect to see a three-tier stand with sandwiches, scones, and desserts on the table. Of course, there will be a pot of hot English tea. The atmosphere will be very elegant and quiet, with some soft music playing in the restaurant.|I think there will be scones, jam, butter, and some small cakes on the table. A pot of good tea is also very important. The atmosphere is very relaxing and nice, people dress beautifully and talk quietly with their friends.
8	I expect to see a pot of hot tea, and a stand with scones, sandwiches, and small cakes. There will also be butter and jam for the scones. The atmosphere will be very quiet, comfortable, and nice for chatting with friends.|There will have many cakes and scones on the table. And a big pot of black tea. The atmosphere is very good and elegant, people can relax and talk together.|I will see tea, sandwiches and scones. English people very like scones with jam. The atmosphere is very happy and relaxing.
7	There will be tea, some cakes, and scones with jam on the table. The atmosphere is very relaxing and nice. People wear nice clothes and talk quietly.|I expect to see black tea and small cakes. There are also sandwiches. The atmosphere is very quiet and comfortable.|The table has tea and cakes. The atmosphere is very good. You can chat with your friends happily.
6	I expect to see black tea, cakes, and sandwiches. The atmosphere is very good and quiet. You can relax there.|There is tea and scones on table. The atmosphere is happy. People talk together.|See many cakes and tea. It is very beautiful. Atmosphere is relaxing.
5	I see tea and cake. Table is beautiful. Atmosphere is happy.|Have tea, sandwich. People is talk. Very relax.|Eat scone and tea. Very good. I happy.
4	Tea, cake. Happy.|Drink tea. Relax.|Eat cake. Beautiful.
3	Tea... sandwich.|Drink... talk.|Cake... happy.
2	Tea.|Cake.|Eat.
1	I don't know.|(Silence)|Hmm...

prompt: Imagine your friend is traveling to the UK for the first time next month. Based on your understanding, what is one important piece of advice you would give them about experiencing the local culture?
reference_answer:
10	My most crucial piece of advice would be to thoroughly understand and respect the British queuing culture. Whether you are waiting for public transportation, purchasing groceries, or using a restroom, you must wait in line with absolute patience. Jumping the queue is universally viewed as highly offensive in the UK, so practicing good manners in this regard is essential for blending in.|I would enthusiastically recommend that my friend visit an authentic local pub to truly absorb the everyday British lifestyle. However, I would remind them that in most pubs, patrons are required to approach the bar to order their own food and beverages. It serves as the ultimate social hub to observe how locals genuinely unwind and interact after work.|One indispensable piece of advice is to be exceptionally mindful of verbal politeness, particularly overusing words like 'please', 'thank you', and 'sorry'. The British place immense value on courtesy in all daily interactions. Furthermore, they must always be prepared for highly unpredictable weather by carrying a compact umbrella, regardless of the morning forecast!
9	I would advise my friend to visit a traditional local pub to experience the true culture. However, they need to remember that in a pub, you usually have to go to the bar to order your food and drinks yourself. It's the best place to see how locals relax and interact with each other.|I will tell them to respect the queuing rules. In the UK, people line up for everything, like waiting for a bus or buying coffee. If you cut in line, people will be very angry, so you must wait patiently.|My advice is to always bring an umbrella when going out. The weather in the UK changes very quickly, it can rain anytime. Also, remember to say please and thank you to be polite to the local people.
8	I would tell my friend to remember to be polite and say 'please', 'thank you', and 'sorry' very often. Good manners are very important in British culture. It will help them make a good impression and make friends easily.|My advice is you must line up everywhere. British people hate people who don't line up. You need to wait for your turn patiently.|I will advise my friend to try afternoon tea. It is a famous British culture. You can eat delicious scones and drink tea like a local person.
7	My advice is to try the traditional afternoon tea because it is very famous. You should eat scones with jam and cream. You can also learn how to drink tea like a local person.|I will tell my friend to bring an umbrella. The weather changes very fast. And remember to say thank you and be polite.|Remember to line up when you buy things or wait for bus. It is a very important rule in the UK. Don't jump the queue.
6	I will tell my friend to always bring an umbrella. The weather in the UK changes very fast. Also, remember to line up when you buy things.|Be polite to people. Say thank you and sorry. And try to drink afternoon tea.|Line up everywhere. It is important. Bring umbrella because it rains.
5	You bring umbrella. Weather is rain. You say thank you.|Line up for buy thing. Be polite. Have fun.|You go eat afternoon tea. It is delicious. Bring umbrella.
4	Bring umbrella. Say sorry.|Line up. Polite.|Drink tea. Rain.
3	Umbrella... rain.|Line up... good.|Tea... drink.
2	Umbrella.|Line up.|Polite.
1	(Silence)|I don't know.|No advice.
